text: This video demonstrates a robotic assisted appendectomy for acute appendicitis with moderate to severe cecal inflammation. With this degree of cecal inflammation, a simple appendectomy procedure may not be sufficient for this patient.

Conservative surgical recommendations might include ileocecectomy. Depending on the surgeon's comfort level with minimally invasive techniques, this may require conversion from a laparoscopic to an open approach.
The surgeon applies an omental gram patch to the appendiceal stump, using a minimally invasive technique.
This patient presented with a three-day history of right lower quadrant pain. CT scan demonstrated a non-perforated appendicitis with extensive cecal inflammation.

Given the patient's physical exam, I was not comfortable with the non-operative approach of antibiotics and bowel rest. We therefore proceeded to the operating room for robotic assisted appendectomy and all indicated procedures.
Three trocars are placed in a vertical line along the left rectus abdominis muscle, with the center incision at the level of the umbilicus, and additional incisions positioned above and below.
In uncomplicated cases, an appendectomy can be performed with a single vicryl suture. I have posted other videos demonstrating this operation.

In this case, however, the cecum is inflamed to an extent that I was not comfortable simply tying or suturing the appendiceal stump, and imbricating into the inflamed cecum would also not be feasible. I did not feel a laparoscopic stapler would provide a better solution, as it would likely crush and fracture the inflamed tissue causing a larger defect in the cecum.

Conservative recommendations in such a case could include ileocecectomy. As mentioned before, this might require an open operation depending on the surgeon's comfort level with robotic or laparoscopic techniques. In addition, sacrificing the ileocecal valve can have substantial consequences for the patient. Depending on age and other comorbidities, this could range from slight irritation to incontinence and dehydration.
The surgeon applies an omental patch over the appendiceal stump to reinforce the closure, using the omentum to cover and protect the area.

A larger resection, such as an ileosecectomy, could negatively impact the patient's lifestyle. In addition, an ileocecctomy may require a moderate midline incision. This results in a longer hospital stay, increased postoperative pain, larger risk of superficial or deep infection, and a higher risk of incisional hernia.

On the opposite side of that coin, inappropriate closure of the appendiceal stump could result in a leak with disastrous outcomes. I find this alternative to be an elegant answer to a potentially complicated problem.

This is a simple technique that is easily performed on the robotic platform. The operation remains robotic without converting to an open technique, and we can preserve the ileocecal valve for this patient.

The surgeon places interrupted vicryl sutures over the purse string suture to reinforce the closure of the appendiceal stump. Two O-silk sutures are positioned at the superior and inferior base of the stump, tying them over the omental patch to achieve solid approximation of the omentum to the inflamed stump.

This patient was discharged home on post-operative day one. Pathology was consistent with a standard appendicitis. In follow-up clinic after two weeks, the patient had a full recovery and was symptom free.
It is also worth noting that as this operation does not require a stapler, it costs less than a standard laparoscopic appendectomy.
I hope these videos have been helpful. Thank you for watching.
